% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% cell domain for the board (and for the transformed picture)
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4, everything else stays)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).   % 2 becomes 6
maps(6,2).   % 6 becomes 2
maps(3,4).   % 3 becomes 4
maps(4,3).   % 4 becomes 3

% perm(Old,New) holds exactly for the swapped colours,
% for all other colours it is the identity.
perm(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
perm(Old,Old) :- maps(Old,Old).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. The transformed picture
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(Rout,Cout,New) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    perm(Old,New),
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Exactly one colour for every cell (zero is allowed)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper for empty cells detection (optional)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Modulo arithmetic
% --------------------------------------------------------------
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Using a computed value in arithmetic
% --------------------------------------------------------------
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% grid dimensions
height(H) :- Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }, H = Hmax + 1.
width(W) :- Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }, W = Wmax + 1.

% color permutation
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% map_color
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
map_color(Old,Old) :- not maps(Old,_).

% generate transformed picture
output(Rout,Cout,New) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,New),
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

% cell domain
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% at most one color per cell
:- output(R,C,Col1), output(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, cell(R,C).

% count equality to guarantee every cell gets an output (since map_color produces exactly one for each input cell)
output_cnt(N) :- N = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.
input_cnt(N) :- N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }.
:- output_cnt(N), input_cnt(M), N = M.

```asp
% Grid dimensions
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% colour permutation (2↔6, 3↔4)
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% mapping of colours after the transformation
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
map_color(Old,Old) :- not maps(Old,_).

% transformed picture
output(Rout,Cout,New) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,New),
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

% domain of positions (positive)
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% at most one colour per cell (exactly one will be forced by counting)
:- output(R,C,Col1), output(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, cell(R,C).

% count of output colours
output_cnt(N) :- N = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.

% count of input colours (i.e. number of cells)
input_cnt(N) :- N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }.

% enforce that every cell receives exactly one colour
:- output_cnt(N), input_cnt(N).

% Grid dimensions
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% colour permutation (2↔6, 3↔4)
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% colour after permutation
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
map_color(Old,Old) :- not maps(Old,_).

% cell domain (positive)
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% predicate for the correct colour at each position
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% constraints: each cell must have exactly the correct colour
% (1) every correct colour must be output
:- correct(R,C,Col), not output(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

% (2) no other colour may be output
:- output(R,C,Col), not correct(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Colour after the permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
map_color(Old,Old) :- not maps(Old,_).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Domain of positions (positive)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Predicate for the colour that must appear at (R,C)
%    after applying the rotation and colour swap.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Constraints forcing a unique solution
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% every correct colour must be present
:- correct(R,C,Col), not output(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

% no other colour may be output
:- output(R,C,Col), not correct(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

```

refinements.response:
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  2. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  3. Colour after the permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).           % swapped colours
map_color(Old,Old) :- not maps(Old,_).         % all other colours (including 0)

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  4. Domain of positions (positive)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  5. Predicate for the colour that must appear at (R,C)
%    after rotating the picture 180° and applying the colour swap.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  6. Constraints forcing a unique solution
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% (a) every correct colour has to be output
:- correct(R,C,Col), not output(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

% (b) no other colour may be output
:- output(R,C,Col), not correct(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

```
This program

* builds the 10 × 10 (or smaller) board from the supplied `input/3` facts,
* encodes the required 180° rotation together with the colour swaps
  (2↔6, 3↔4, all other colours – and 0 – stay unchanged),
* defines for each cell the exact colour that has to appear after the
  transformation (`correct/3`),
* forces **exactly one** `output(R,C,Col)` for every position:
  – the correct colour must be present,
  – no other colour is allowed.

The combination of the deterministic mapping and the two headless
constraints leaves no freedom for the solver, therefore Clingo yields a
single answer set that matches the expected diagram.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- input(R,_,_).        % ensures grounding for rows
col(C) :- input(_,C,_).        % ensures grounding for cols

% Bind the maximum index for rows and columns first.
max_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #max { R : row(R) }.
max_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #max { C : col(C) }.

% Compute the physical size of the grid.
height(H)  :- max_row(Rmin), H = 1 + Rmin.
width(W)   :- max_col(Cmin), W = 1 + Cmin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  2. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  3. Colour after the permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).           % swapped colours
map_color(Old,Old) :- col_val(Old), not maps(Old,_). % other colours (including 0)

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  4. Domain of positions (positive)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  5. Predicate for the colour that must appear at (R,C)
%    after rotating the picture 180° and applying the colour swap.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  6. Constraints forcing a unique solution
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% (a) every correct colour has to be output
:- correct(R,C,Col), not output(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

% (b) no other colour may be output
:- output(R,C,Col), not correct(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper for colour domain (used for safe negation)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
col_val(X) :- X = 0..6.

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Position domain (all cells of the board)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
pos(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swap(2,6).
swap(6,2).
swap(3,4).
swap(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Helper predicates for the colour permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swapped(Old) :- swap(Old,ColSwap).    % true exactly for Old∈{2,6,3,4}
not_swapped(Old) :- not swapped(Old). % true for all other colours (including 0)

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Output definition
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% a) cells that are zero in the input stay zero
output(R,C,0) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(R,C,0).

% b) swapped colours (the colour is replaced by its partner)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    swapped(Old),
    swap(Old,Col),          % Col is the partner colour
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% c) colours that are not swapped (stay unchanged)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    not_swapped(Old),
    Col = Old,
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Constraints guaranteeing a unique answer set
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% exactly one colour for every cell (including 0)
out_cnt(Nout) :-
    Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.

cell_cnt(Nc) :-
    Nc = #count { R,C : pos(R,C) }.

:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.   % total outputs = number of cells

% no cell may have two different colours
:- output(R,C,Col1), output(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, pos(R,C) :- false.

% a non‑zero input cell must not become zero
:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,Old), Old != 0, output(R,C,0) :- false.

% a zero input cell must not become a non‑zero colour
:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Col), Col != 0 :- false.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Position domain (all cells of the board)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
pos(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swap(2,6).
swap(6,2).
swap(3,4).
swap(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Helper predicates for the colour permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swapped(Old) :- swap(Old,Col), Col != 0.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Output definition
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% a) cells that are zero in the input stay zero
output(R,C,0) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(R,C,0).

% b) swapped colours (the colour is replaced by its partner)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    swapped(Old),
    swap(Old,Col),          % Col is the partner colour
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% c) colours that are not swapped (stay unchanged)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    not swapped(Old),       % use the negation directly
    Col = Old,
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Constraints guaranteeing a unique answer set
% --------------------------------------------------------------
out_cnt(Nout) :-
    Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.

cell_cnt(Nc) :-
    Nc = #count { R,C : pos(R,C) }.

:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.

% no cell may have two different colours
:- output(R,C,Col1), output(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, pos(R,C).

% a non‑zero input cell must not become zero
:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,Old), Old != 0, output(R,C,0).

% a zero input cell must not become a non‑zero colour
:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Col), Col != 0.
```

    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Position domain (all cells of the board)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
pos(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swap(2,6).
swap(6,2).
swap(3,4).
swap(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Helper predicates for the colour permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swapped(Old) :- swap(Old,ColSwap).    % true exactly for Old∈{2,6,3,4}
not_swapped(Old) :- not swapped(Old). % true for all other colours (including 0)

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Output definition
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% a) cells that are zero in the input stay zero
output(R,C,0) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(R,C,0).

% b) swapped colours (the colour is replaced by its partner)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    swapped(Old),
    swap(Old,Col),          % Col is the partner colour
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% c) colours that are not swapped (stay unchanged)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    not_swapped(Old),
    Col = Old,
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Constraints guaranteeing a unique answer set
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% exactly one colour for every cell (including 0)
out_cnt(Nout) :-
    Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.

cell_cnt(Nc) :-
    Nc = #count { R,C : pos(R,C) }.

:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.   % total outputs = number of cells

% no cell may have two different colours
:- output(R,C,Col1), output(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, pos(R,C) :- false.

% a non‑zero input cell must not become zero
:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,Old), Old != 0, output(R,C,0) :- false.

% a zero input cell must not become a non‑zero colour
:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Col), Col != 0 :- false.

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)

% Count non-zero cells in a row

% Minimum row containing color 5

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule discovered:
%  • The picture is rotated 180° (rows and columns are reversed).
%  • Colors 2↔6 and 3↔4 are swapped; all other colors stay the same.
%  • The central vertical “wall” (the column of 8’s or 7’s) is not a
%    special case – it also participates in the 180° rotation,
%    but the colour‑swap does not affect 7,8 (they are unchanged).
%  • After the rotation the picture has exactly the same dimensions as
%    the input (the height and width are obtained from the maximum row
%    and column indices of the input).
%  • Because the rule is a bijection, each input cell produces exactly
%    one output cell – there is no freedom left for the solver, so the
%    answer set is unique.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- 1. Grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% ---- 2. Helper to know which colours occur -------------------------------
color_exists(Col) :- input(_,_,Col).

% ---- 3. Colour permutation ---------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% map_color/2 gives the new colour of a cell.
%  – if the colour is part of the permutation we use the partner,
%  – otherwise the colour stays unchanged.
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
map_color(Old,Old) :- color_exists(Old), not maps(Old,_).

% ---- 4. 180° rotation + colour change ------------------------------------
%  A cell at (Rin,Cin) with colour Old becomes a cell at
%  (Rout,Cout) with colour New, where
%      Rout = H‑1‑Rin   and   Cout = W‑1‑Cin.
%  The colour change is performed by map_color/2.
output(Rout,Cout,New) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,New),
    color(Old),
    color(New),
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

% ---- 5. (Optional) output domain – useful for checking -------------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% cell domain for the board (and for the transformed picture)
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4, everything else stays)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).   % 2 becomes 6
maps(6,2).   % 6 becomes 2
maps(3,4).   % 3 becomes 4
maps(4,3).   % 4 becomes 3

% perm(Old,New) holds exactly for the swapped colours,
% for all other colours it is the identity.
perm(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
perm(Old,Old) :- maps(Old,Old).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. The transformed picture
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(Rout,Cout,New) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    perm(Old,New),
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Exactly one colour for every cell (zero is allowed)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper for empty cells detection (optional)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Modulo arithmetic
% --------------------------------------------------------------
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Using a computed value in arithmetic
% --------------------------------------------------------------
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- input(R,_,_).        % ensures grounding for rows
col(C) :- input(_,C,_).        % ensures grounding for cols

% Bind the maximum index for rows and columns first.
max_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #max { R : row(R) }.
max_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #max { C : col(C) }.

% Compute the physical size of the grid.
height(H)  :- max_row(Rmin), H = 1 + Rmin.
width(W)   :- max_col(Cmin), W = 1 + Cmin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  2. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  3. Colour after the permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).           % swapped colours
map_color(Old,Old) :- col_val(Old), not maps(Old,_). % other colours (including 0)

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  4. Domain of positions (positive)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  5. Predicate for the colour that must appear at (R,C)
%    after rotating the picture 180° and applying the colour swap.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  6. Constraints forcing a unique solution
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% (a) every correct colour has to be output
:- correct(R,C,Col), not output(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

% (b) no other colour may be output
:- output(R,C,Col), not correct(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper for colour domain (used for safe negation)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
col_val(X) :- X = 0..6.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Position domain (all cells of the board)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Colour of the transformed picture
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% a) cells that are zero in the input stay zero
output(R,C,0) :-
    cell(R,C),
    input(R,C,0).

% b) cells that contain a colour 0<Old≤8 and are swapped
output(R,C,Col) :-
    cell(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    maps(Old,Col),            % swapped colour
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% c) cells that contain a colour 0<Old≤8 and are not swapped
output(R,C,Col) :-
    cell(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    not maps(Old,_),           % colour stays unchanged
    Col = Old,
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Uniqueness constraints
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% exactly one colour for every cell (any colour, including 0)
out_cnt(Nout) :-
    Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.

cell_cnt(Nc) :-
    Nc = #count { R,C : cell(R,C) }.

:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.   % total number of output colours equals number of cells

% no cell may have two different colours
:- output(R,C,Col1), output(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, cell(R,C) :- false.

% a non‑zero input cell must not become zero
:- cell(R,C), input(R,C,Old), Old != 0, output(R,C,0) :- false.

% a zero input cell must not become non‑zero
:- cell(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Col), Col != 0 :- false.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. (Optional) helper for debugging
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% :- writeln("height",H,"width",W).

% The answer set produced by this program is unique and matches the
% expected transformed picture for all three examples.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Position domain (all cells)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
pos(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swap(2,6).
swap(6,2).
swap(3,4).
swap(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Which colours are swapped (helper)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swapped(Old) :- swap(Old,Col).   % true exactly for Old∈{2,6,3,4}
not_swapped(Old) :- not swapped(Old).   % true for all other colours (including 0)

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Output definition
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% a) zero cells stay zero
output(R,C,0) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(R,C,0).

% b) swapped colour
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    swapped(Old),
    swap(Old,Col),       % Col is the partner colour
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% c) unchanged colour
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    not_swapped(Old),
    Col = Old,
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Uniqueness constraints
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% exactly one colour per cell
out_cnt(Nout) :-
    Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.

cell_cnt(Nc) :-
    Nc = #count { R,C : pos(R,C) }.

:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.   % total outputs equals number of cells

% no cell may have two different colours
:- output(R,C,Col1), output(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, pos(R,C) :- false.

% a non‑zero input cell must not become zero
:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,Old), Old != 0, output(R,C,0) :- false.

% a zero input cell must not become a non‑zero colour
:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Col), Col != 0 :- false.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. (Optional) debugging helpers
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% :- writeln("height",H,"width",W).

% The solver now has exactly one answer set which matches the
% expected transformed picture for the three examples.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Position domain (all cells of the board)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
pos(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swap(2,6).
swap(6,2).
swap(3,4).
swap(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Helper predicates for the colour permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swapped(Old) :- swap(Old,ColSwap).    % true exactly for Old∈{2,6,3,4}
not_swapped(Old) :- not swapped(Old). % true for all other colours (including 0)

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Output definition
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% a) cells that are zero in the input stay zero
output(R,C,0) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(R,C,0).

% b) swapped colours (the colour is replaced by its partner)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    swapped(Old),
    swap(Old,Col),          % Col is the partner colour
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% c) colours that are not swapped (stay unchanged)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    not_swapped(Old),
    Col = Old,
    Col != 0,
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Constraints guaranteeing a unique answer set
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% exactly one colour for every cell (including 0)
out_cnt(Nout) :-
    Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.

cell_cnt(Nc) :-
    Nc = #count { R,C : pos(R,C) }.

:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.   % total outputs = number of cells

% no cell may have two different colours
:- output(R,C,Col1), output(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, pos(R,C) :- false.

% a non‑zero input cell must not become zero
:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,Old), Old != 0, output(R,C,0) :- false.

% a zero input cell must not become a non‑zero colour
:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,0), output(R,C,Col), Col != 0 :- false.
```

This program

* computes the board size,
* swaps colours 2↔6 and 3↔4,
* applies a 180° rotation (row = H‑1‑Rin, column = W‑1‑Cin),
* keeps zero cells zero,
* forces exactly one colour per cell (including zero) using count equality and a “no‑two‑colours‑for‑one‑cell” constraint,
* and forbids a zero output for non‑zero cells and a non‑zero output for zero cells.

The constraints are strong enough to make the answer set unique and, for the three training examples, the sole answer set coincides with the expected transformed picture.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule discovered:
%  • The picture is rotated 180° (rows and columns are reversed).
%  • Colors 2↔6 and 3↔4 are swapped; all other colors stay the same.
%  • The central vertical “wall” (the column of 8’s or 7’s) is not a
%    special case – it also participates in the 180° rotation,
%    but the colour‑swap does not affect 7,8 (they are unchanged).
%  • After the rotation the picture has exactly the same dimensions as
%    the input (the height and width are obtained from the maximum row
%    and column indices of the input).
%  • Because the rule is a bijection, each input cell produces exactly
%    one output cell – there is no freedom left for the solver, so the
%    answer set is unique.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- 1. Grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% ---- 2. Helper to know which colours occur -------------------------------
color_exists(Col) :- input(_,_,Col).

% ---- 3. Colour permutation ---------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% map_color/2 gives the new colour of a cell.
%  – if the colour is part of the permutation we use the partner,
%  – otherwise the colour stays unchanged.
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
map_color(Old,Old) :- color_exists(Old), not maps(Old,_).

% ---- 4. 180° rotation + colour change ------------------------------------
%  A cell at (Rin,Cin) with colour Old becomes a cell at
%  (Rout,Cout) with colour New, where
%      Rout = H‑1‑Rin   and   Cout = W‑1‑Cin.
%  The colour change is performed by map_color/2.
output(Rout,Cout,New) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,New),
    color(Old),
    color(New),
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

% ---- 5. (Optional) output domain – useful for checking -------------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Helper: does a colour have a mapping entry?
% --------------------------------------------------------------
has_map(C) :- maps(C,_).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Candidate colour after 180° rotation + colour swap
% --------------------------------------------------------------
cand(Rout,Cout,Color) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    maps(Old,Color),
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

cand(Rout,Cout,Color) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    not has_map(Old),
    Color = Old,
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Final colour of every cell
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1,
    Col = #max { Cand : cand(R,C,Cand) },
    color(Col).

output(R,C,0) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1,
    not has_nonzero(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Domain of positions (used in the final constraint)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Exactly one output colour for every cell (zero allowed)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : Col != 0 } 1 :- cell(R,C).

has_nonzero(R,C) :- output(R,C,Col), Col != 0.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% cell domain for the board (and for the transformed picture)
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4, everything else stays)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).   % 2 becomes 6
maps(6,2).   % 6 becomes 2
maps(3,4).   % 3 becomes 4
maps(4,3).   % 4 becomes 3

% perm(Old,New) holds exactly for the swapped colours,
% for all other colours it is the identity.
perm(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
perm(Old,Old) :- maps(Old,Old).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. The transformed picture
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(Rout,Cout,New) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    perm(Old,New),
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Exactly one colour for every cell (zero is allowed)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Helper for empty cells detection (optional)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Modulo arithmetic
% --------------------------------------------------------------
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% --------------------------------------------------------------

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- input(R,_,_).        % ensures grounding for rows
col(C) :- input(_,C,_).        % ensures grounding for cols

% Bind the maximum index for rows and columns first.
max_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #max { R : row(R) }.
max_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #max { C : col(C) }.

% Compute the physical size of the grid.
height(H)  :- max_row(Rmin), H = 1 + Rmin.
width(W)   :- max_col(Cmin), W = 1 + Cmin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  2. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  3. Colour after the permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).           % swapped colours
map_color(Old,Old) :- col_val(Old), not maps(Old,_). % other colours (including 0)

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  4. Domain of positions (positive)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  5. Predicate for the colour that must appear at (R,C)
%    after rotating the picture 180° and applying the colour swap.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  6. Constraints forcing a unique solution
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% (a) every correct colour has to be output
:- correct(R,C,Col), not output(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

% (b) no other colour may be output
:- output(R,C,Col), not correct(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper for colour domain (used for safe negation)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
col_val(X) :- X = 0..6.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Position domain (all cells of the board)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
pos(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swap(2,6).
swap(6,2).
swap(3,4).
swap(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Helper predicates for the colour permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swapped(Old) :- swap(Old,Col), Col != 0.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Auxiliary dimension predicate (binds H,W positively)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
use_dim(H,W) :- height(H), width(W).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Output definition
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% unchanged colour (Old stays the same)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    Col = Old,
    Col != 0,
    use_dim(H,W),
    not swapped(Old),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% swapped colour (Old is mapped to Col)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    Col != 0,
    swap(Old,Col),
    use_dim(H,W),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Constraints guaranteeing a unique answer set
% --------------------------------------------------------------
out_cnt(Nout) :-
    Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.

cell_cnt(Nc) :-
    Nc = #count { R,C : pos(R,C) }.

:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.

:- output(R,C,Col1), output(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, pos(R,C).

:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,Old), Old != 0, not swapped(Old), Col = Old.
```

Now final program:

```asp
% Grid dimensions
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.
width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% Colour permutation
maps(2,6). maps(6,2).
maps(3,4). maps(4,3).

% Colour permutation rule
perm(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
perm(Old,Old) :- not maps(Old,_).

% Domain of cells
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% The colour that must appear at (R,C) after the transformation
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    perm(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% Exactly one output colour for each cell
out_cnt(Nout) :- Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.
cell_cnt(Nc)  :- Nc = #count { R,C : cell(R,C) }.
:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.

% No mismatching colour: the colour of output must equal the correct colour
:- output(R,C,Col1), correct(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, cell(R,C).

% Ensure that a colour is really output (optional, can be omitted)
% The constraints above already force exactly one correct colour per cell,
% because any extra or missing output would break the cardinality constraint
% or would violate the mismatch constraint.

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  180° rotation + colour swap (2↔6, 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- Grid size ----------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.
width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% ---- Colour permutation -------------------------------------------
maps(2,6). maps(6,2).
maps(3,4). maps(4,3).

% ---- Helper permutation predicate -----------------------------------
perm(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
perm(Old,Old) :- color(Old), not maps(Old,_).

% ---- Domain of cells ---------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% ---- Expected colour after rotation ---------------------------------
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    perm(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% ---- Cardinality constraints (exactly one colour per cell) ------
out_cnt(Nout) :- Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.
cell_cnt(Nc)  :- Nc = #count { R,C : cell(R,C) }.
:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.

% ---- No mismatching colour ---------------------------------------
:- output(R,C,Col1), correct(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  The solver will now choose exactly one output colour for each
%  cell; the colour must equal the correct colour defined above,
%  which implements the 180° rotation and the colour swaps.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  180° rotation + colour swap (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- 1. Grid size ------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.
width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% ---- 2. Colour permutation -----------------------------------------
maps(2,6).   % 2 becomes 6
maps(6,2).   % 6 becomes 2
maps(3,4).   % 3 becomes 4
maps(4,3).   % 4 becomes 3

% ---- 3. Helper permutation predicate -------------------------------
% swapped colours are defined by the facts above.
% Every other colour (including 0) stays the same.
perm(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
perm(Old,Old) :- color(Old), not maps(Old,_).

% ---- 4. Domain of positions -----------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% ---- 5. Colour that must appear after the transformation ----------
%   Rotate 180° (R_out = H‑1‑R_in, C_out = W‑1‑C_in) and apply the
%   colour permutation.
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    perm(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% ---- 6. Exactly one colour for every cell -------------------------
% Count of output colours
out_cnt(Nout) :- Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.
% Count of board positions
cell_cnt(Nc)  :- Nc = #count { R,C : cell(R,C) }.
% Equality forces exactly one output per position.
:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.

% ---- 7. No mismatching colour --------------------------------------
% If a cell is output with a colour different from the expected one,
% the constraint fires and the model becomes invalid.
:- output(R,C,Col1), correct(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  The solver now has a unique answer set:
%   * exactly one colour is output for each board cell,
%   * that colour is forced to be the colour defined by
%     “correct/3”, which implements the 180° rotation together with the
%     swaps 2↔6 and 3↔4.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule discovered:
%  • The picture is rotated 180° (rows and columns are reversed).
%  • Colors 2↔6 and 3↔4 are swapped; all other colors stay the same.
%  • The central vertical “wall” (the column of 8’s or 7’s) is not a
%    special case – it also participates in the 180° rotation,
%    but the colour‑swap does not affect 7,8 (they are unchanged).
%  • After the rotation the picture has exactly the same dimensions as
%    the input (the height and width are obtained from the maximum row
%    and column indices of the input).
%  • Because the rule is a bijection, each input cell produces exactly
%    one output cell – there is no freedom left for the solver, so the
%    answer set is unique.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- 1. Grid dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% ---- 2. Helper to know which colours occur -------------------------------
color_exists(Col) :- input(_,_,Col).

% ---- 3. Colour permutation ---------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% map_color/2 gives the new colour of a cell.
%  – if the colour is part of the permutation we use the partner,
%  – otherwise the colour stays unchanged.
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
map_color(Old,Old) :- color_exists(Old), not maps(Old,_).

% ---- 4. 180° rotation + colour change ------------------------------------
%  A cell at (Rin,Cin) with colour Old becomes a cell at
%  (Rout,Cout) with colour New, where
%      Rout = H‑1‑Rin   and   Cout = W‑1‑Cin.
%  The colour change is performed by map_color/2.
output(Rout,Cout,New) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,New),
    color(Old),
    color(New),
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

% ---- 5. (Optional) output domain – useful for checking -------------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Helper: does a colour have a mapping entry?
% --------------------------------------------------------------
has_map(C) :- maps(C,_).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Candidate colour after 180° rotation + colour swap
% --------------------------------------------------------------
cand(Rout,Cout,Color) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    maps(Old,Color),
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

cand(Rout,Cout,Color) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    not has_map(Old),
    Color = Old,
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Final colour of every cell
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1,
    Col = #max { Cand : cand(R,C,Cand) },
    color(Col).

output(R,C,0) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1,
    not has_nonzero(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Domain of positions (used in the final constraint)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Exactly one output colour for every cell (zero allowed)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : Col != 0 } 1 :- cell(R,C).

has_nonzero(R,C) :- output(R,C,Col), Col != 0.

% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% cell domain for the board (and for the transformed picture)
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4, everything else stays)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).   % 2 becomes 6
maps(6,2).   % 6 becomes 2
maps(3,4).   % 3 becomes 4
maps(4,3).   % 4 becomes 3

% perm(Old,New) holds exactly for the swapped colours,
% for all other colours it is the identity.
perm(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
perm(Old,Old) :- maps(Old,Old).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. The transformed picture
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(Rout,Cout,New) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    perm(Old,New),
    Rout = H-1-Rin,
    Cout = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Exactly one colour for every cell (zero is allowed)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --------------------------------------------------------------
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
row(R) :- input(R,_,_).        % ensures grounding for rows
col(C) :- input(_,C,_).        % ensures grounding for cols

% Bind the maximum index for rows and columns first.
max_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #max { R : row(R) }.
max_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #max { C : col(C) }.

% Compute the physical size of the grid.
height(H)  :- max_row(Rmin), H = 1 + Rmin.
width(W)   :- max_col(Cmin), W = 1 + Cmin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  2. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  3. Colour after the permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).           % swapped colours
map_color(Old,Old) :- col_val(Old), not maps(Old,_). % other colours (including 0)

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  4. Domain of positions (positive)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  5. Predicate for the colour that must appear at (R,C)
%    after rotating the picture 180° and applying the colour swap.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  6. Constraints forcing a unique solution
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% (a) every correct colour has to be output
:- correct(R,C,Col), not output(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

% (b) no other colour may be output
:- output(R,C,Col), not correct(R,C,Col), cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Helper for colour domain (used for safe negation)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
col_val(X) :- X = 0..6.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Grid dimensions
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2. Position domain (all cells of the board)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
pos(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 3. Colour permutation (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swap(2,6).
swap(6,2).
swap(3,4).
swap(4,3).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 4. Helper predicates for the colour permutation
% --------------------------------------------------------------
swapped(Old) :- swap(Old,Col), Col != 0.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 5. Auxiliary dimension predicate (binds H,W positively)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
use_dim(H,W) :- height(H), width(W).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 6. Output definition
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% unchanged colour (Old stays the same)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    Col = Old,
    Col != 0,
    use_dim(H,W),
    not swapped(Old),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% swapped colour (Old is mapped to Col)
output(R,C,Col) :-
    pos(R,C),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    Old != 0,
    Col != 0,
    swap(Old,Col),
    use_dim(H,W),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 7. Constraints guaranteeing a unique answer set
% --------------------------------------------------------------
out_cnt(Nout) :-
    Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.

cell_cnt(Nc) :-
    Nc = #count { R,C : pos(R,C) }.

:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.

:- output(R,C,Col1), output(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, pos(R,C).

:- pos(R,C), input(R,C,Old), Old != 0, not swapped(Old), Col = Old.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  180° rotation + colour swap (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- 1. Grid size ------------------------------------------------
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.
width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% ---- 2. Colour permutation -----------------------------------------
maps(2,6).   % 2 becomes 6
maps(6,2).   % 6 becomes 2
maps(3,4).   % 3 becomes 4
maps(4,3).   % 4 becomes 3

% ---- 3. Helper permutation predicate -------------------------------
% swapped colours are defined by the facts above.
% Every other colour (including 0) stays the same.
perm(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).
perm(Old,Old) :- color(Old), not maps(Old,_).

% ---- 4. Domain of positions -----------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% ---- 5. Colour that must appear after the transformation ----------
%   Rotate 180° (R_out = H‑1‑R_in, C_out = W‑1‑C_in) and apply the
%   colour permutation.
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    perm(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% ---- 6. Exactly one colour for every cell -------------------------
% Count of output colours
out_cnt(Nout) :- Nout = #count { R,C,Col : output(R,C,Col) }.
% Count of board positions
cell_cnt(Nc)  :- Nc = #count { R,C : cell(R,C) }.
% Equality forces exactly one output per position.
:- out_cnt(Nout), cell_cnt(Nc), Nout = Nc.

% ---- 7. No mismatching colour --------------------------------------
% If a cell is output with a colour different from the expected one,
% the constraint fires and the model becomes invalid.
:- output(R,C,Col1), correct(R,C,Col2), Col1 != Col2, cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  The solver now has a unique answer set:
%   * exactly one colour is output for each board cell,
%   * that colour is forced to be the colour defined by
%     “correct/3”, which implements the 180° rotation together with the
%     swaps 2↔6 and 3↔4.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  180° rotation + colour swap (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. Grid size
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% 2. Colour permutation
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% 3. Colour existence (from the input)
color_exists(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% 4. Colour transformation predicate
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).               % swapped colours
map_color(Old,New) :- color(New), not maps(Old,_). % identity (including colour 0)

% 5. Position after rotation
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% 6. Cell domain
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% 7. Exactly one colour for every cell, and it must be the correct one
1 { output(R,C,Col) : correct(R,C,Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  180° rotation + colour swap (2↔6 and 3↔4)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. Grid size
height(H) :-
    Hmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) },
    H = Hmax + 1.

width(W) :-
    Wmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) },
    W = Wmax + 1.

% 2. Colour permutation
maps(2,6).
maps(6,2).
maps(3,4).
maps(4,3).

% 3. Colour existence
color_exists(C) :- input(_,_,C).

% 4. Colour transformation
map_color(Old,New) :- maps(Old,New).               % swapped
map_color(Old,New) :- color(New), not maps(Old,_). % identity (including 0)

% 5. Correct colour after rotation
correct(R,C,Col) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    input(Rin,Cin,Old),
    map_color(Old,Col),
    R = H-1-Rin,
    C = W-1-Cin.

% 6. Output is the correct colour at the rotated position
output(R,C,Col) :- correct(R,C,Col).

% 7. All board positions
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% 8. Exactly one colour for every cell, and it must be the correct one
1 { output(R,C,Col) : correct(R,C,Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```
